course_description:
Biochemical and biomolecular engineering principles, biomolecular reactions, kinetics and industrial applications. Analysis of engineered biomolecular systems. Design principles of biomolecular production. Downstream bioprocessing including solid-liquid separations, cell disruption, product concentration & purification, recombinant protein refolding and recovery, product stabilisation and formulation. Scale-up considerations.

This course applies chemical and bioprocess engineering principles to bioreactor design and operation. Two central themes are cell growth and biopharmaceutical production. The course covers aspects of Bioreaction engineering and design, processing including upstream and downstream, and analysis. The course first covers types of biomolecules and methods of design, before addressing the production and processing of molecules using cells, bioreactors, and downstream recovery units. Downstream processing, also known as recovery, is addressed in detail including solid-liquid separations, cell disruption, product concentration and purification operations and design, recombinant protein recovery, product stabilisation and formulation. Analytical methods for biomolecules are also covered to ensure product form and efficacy. A series of industry guest lectures will be made to show how the different course elements integrate to enable the engineer to design and optimise the entire process in bioprocess engineering.

course_aims: This course applies chemical engineering principles to biological processes. It includes intra- and extracellular enzyme reactions, stoichiometry, kinetics, immobilisation, bioreactor design and monitoring for various configurations, downstream processing and industrial applications. A brief explanation of microbiology and microbial kinetics is given to allow analysis of fermentation processes, fermentor design & operational principles. Downstream processing of biologically derived products is examined including biotechnological solid-liquid separations, product concentration & purification operations and design. It is of importance to any engineer working in the large scale bioprocessing area.

learning_outcomes.description: Integrate knowledge of biochemistry, microbial growth, enzyme kinetics and biotechnology for analysing the performance of bioreactor processes.
Critically assess bioprocessing applications based on analytical data and how they can be optimised.
Apply bioprocessing design principles to fermentation, sterilisation and downstream processing equipment.
Operate and monitor bioreactor processes hands-on.

assessment_summary.title: Exam In-Semester Outside Scheduled Class Time

assessment_details.title: Fortnightly problem set
assessment_details.task_description: A set of problems that covers course content addressed in that fortnight will be performed regularly throughout the course. Feedback will be provided. The 20% grade encompasses 4% per weekly tutorial submission (6 in total), where the worst grade received on the problem sets will not be counted towards this mark. One of these problem sets will be devoted to a brief report regarding the field trip to Thermofisher/Patheon (further details to be provided). The due dates for the problem sets will be: 17/03, 31/03, 14/04, 6/05, 23/05 and 30/05 (last day of classes). The time each problem set will be due is 4pm. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Problem sets are due following the tutorial every fortnight. They can be submitted online via Turnitin on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Results for problem sets are usually returned after 7 days. If you are granted an exemption, the weighting of the remaining problem sets will be scaled up to equal 20 % in total.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
Assessments must be submitted on or before the due date. Late submissions of assessment items will only be accepted if approval for late submission has been obtained prior to the due date.
Penalties Apply for Late Submission
Refer PPL Assessment Procedure Section 3 Part C (48)
A Student Access Plan (SAP) can only be used for a first extension. Extensions based on an SAP may be granted for up to seven (7) days, or the maximum number of days specified in the Course Instance (CI), if it is less than seven (7) days. Any further extensions will require additional supporting documentation, such as a medical certificate.

assessment_details.title: Exam In-Semester Outside Scheduled Class Time
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be responsible for content delivered in the lectures and tutorials. The basic format of the exam involves multiple choice, short answer and problem solving questions. Students will be invigilated. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Tutorials and in-class exercises Tutorial problems and in-class exercises associated with each week's lecture content. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 2 (24 Feb - 09 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Module 1 - Introduction Introduction to the course and the background principles of bioprocess engineering. Enzyme kinetics and inhibition. Learning outcomes: L01

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 6 (10 Mar - 06 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Module 2 - Stoichiometry and Kinetics Cell metabolism, stoichiometric mass balance concepts, Monod kinetics, cell maintenance, introduction to metabolic flux analysis. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 7 To Week 8 (07 Apr - 20 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Module 3 - Bioreactor design Bioreactor design principles and concepts for different bioreactor configurations. Growth of bacteria vs mammalian cells, mass transfer limitations. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 13 (28 Apr - 01 Jun)
learning_activities.topic: Module 4 - Industrial applications Application of bioprocess engineering: industry guest lectures, biopharmaceuticals, up/down stream processing. Learning outcomes: L03

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 10 (28 Apr - 11 May)
learning_activities.topic: Prac activities Two pracs will be conducted, involving operating lab-scale bioreactors and monitoring them through assessing the biomass growth rate, as well as the oxygen transfer and uptake rate and other relevant process parameters. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Field Trip A guided field trip will take place to Thermofisher/Patheon in 37 Kent St, Woolloongabba QLD 4102, with an accompanying report. The class will split into 2 groups and the tour will be 2h. Learning outcomes: L02, L03

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . School guidelines Your school has additional guidelines you'll need to follow for this course: Safety Induction for Practicals Course guidelines Anyone undertaking courses with a practical component must complete the UQ Undergraduate Student Laboratory Safety Induction and pass the associated assessment. Specific instructions, usage guidelines and rules for each of the undergraduate laboratories will be delivered as part of each course. In some cases, students may be required to attend a specific face-to-face laboratory induction/training session. Learning activities
